system: You are a marketing copy writer. Match the requested length and audience tone.
prompt: Write a 250-300 word product blurb for a developer tool called 'Veridict' — a CI plugin that flags flaky tests by analyzing historical pass/fail patterns and signal correlation across PRs. Audience: engineering managers at mid-sized SaaS companies. Tone: confident, specific, no fluffy adjectives, lead with a quantified problem.
rubric: Pass: 250-300 word range, leads with a quantified problem (numbers/percentages), names the product, mentions historical pattern analysis or PR signal correlation. Avoids generic adjectives ('amazing', 'powerful', 'revolutionary'). Specific enough that an EM could decide to evaluate it.

system: You are a faithful summarizer. Capture the key points; do not invent.
rubric: Pass: exactly 3 bullets, each captures a distinct major point, no invented facts. Order should follow the article's argumentative structure.

system: You are an editor. Rewrite to change tone without changing intent or meaning.
prompt:
Rewrite this email to be more polite and professional, but keep the same core requests:

---
Subject: This is broken

The deploy script broke again. Third time this week. I'm tired of fixing your messes. Fix it before EOD or I'm escalating to leadership. Also your PR from Tuesday still needs review and you've been ignoring me on Slack.

---

Return only the rewritten email body (with subject line).
rubric: Pass: tone is professional/polite, all 3 original requests preserved (fix deploy, review the PR, respond on Slack), no fabricated details. Slight softening is fine; complete removal of urgency = partial.

system: You are an executive assistant. Extract structure from messy notes.
prompt:
Convert these meeting notes into a structured meeting agenda with sections (Discussion, Decisions, Action Items). Keep the meeting title 'Mobile launch sync — 2026-04-23'.

---
mobile launch sync 4/23. ali: ios build still failing on m1 but works on m2 - weird. need to investigate. dec: paul will do that by friday. ben: app store review is taking 2 days longer than usual, we should pad timelines. dec: launch slipped to may 12. amanda wants marketing copy by may 1. unresolved: do we ship dark mode in v1 or v2? need design input. ali to ping nat. paul: should we add crash reporting before launch? everyone said yes. paul will pick a vendor. revenue forecast for q3 was discussed - tabled until next week with cfo.
---
rubric: Pass: 3 clearly-labeled sections (Discussion / Decisions / Action Items), action items have owner + due date where given, dark mode listed as unresolved, revenue forecast deferred. No invented owners or dates.

system: You are a science communicator. Use analogies a 10-year-old can grasp; avoid technical jargon unless you immediately explain it.
prompt: Explain how a transformer language model 'attends' to context, in 200 words or less. Audience: a curious 10-year-old. Use one concrete analogy. Don't say 'matrix' or 'tensor'. Don't say 'I' or 'as an AI'.
rubric: Pass: ≤200 words, includes one concrete analogy, no banned terms (matrix/tensor/I/AI). Captures the core idea (model decides which earlier words matter for the next word). No hallucinated technical facts.

system: You are an editor following user feedback iteratively. The user's first message has the original draft; subsequent messages have feedback. Each reply should be the new revised draft only — no commentary, no preamble.
prompt:
Original draft:

'Our latest release brings a multitude of new and exciting features that we are confident our valued customers will love. With innovative new tools, enhanced performance, and a beautiful redesigned UI, this release represents a major step forward in our journey of continuous improvement. We worked tirelessly to ensure quality and we hope you enjoy.'

Feedback round 1: cut filler words; remove all marketing puffery; keep only concrete claims; under 50 words. Return ONLY the revised draft.
rubric: Pass: ≤50 words, removes 'multitude', 'exciting', 'innovative', 'beautiful', 'tirelessly', 'journey', 'continuous improvement', etc. Keeps only what's concretely stated (new tools, better performance, redesigned UI — to the extent these are concrete). No commentary or preamble around the draft.
